segments.text:
Lab 7 Outline
Sign up for time to meet with Dr. Clark to talk about your journal article
Review Meiosis and allelic segregation
Fern life cycle – observe stages of life cycle, study meiosis, begin genetic project
Figure out patterns of inheritance for novel traits using Classical Genetics Simulator

Assignments due by end of lab:
CGS problem 1 (15 points)
Handout in canvas module
Ceratopteris fern project 1 (15 points)
Handout in canvas module

Meiosis:
Key features: reductive cell division – diploid to haploid.
Chromosomes vs. chromatids
Two rounds of cell division: Meiosis I and II
Meiosis I: Main events-
Rearrangement of alleles on chromosomes.
Tetrad and crossing over.
Separation of (rearranged) homologous 	chromosomes.
Results in 2 haploid cells
Meiosis II: Main events-
Sister chromatids separated (resembles 	mitosis)
DNA copies of each chromosome separated
Ends with 4 haploid cells
Mitosis
Meiosis I
Meiosis II
Meiosis with crossing over

Meiosis Review Complete Table 1 on pg. 7-2
One pair of homologous chromosomes
Draw Prophase I, Metaphase I, Anaphase I, Telophase I
draw what the final cells look like after Telophase II and cytokinesis
Two pairs of homologous chromosomes
Draw Prophase I, Metaphase I, Anaphase I, Telophase I
draw what the final cells look like after Telophase II and cytokinesis
G1 phase
G1 phase
Genotype Ee
Genotype EeGg

Review meiosis & allelic segregation
Starting cell:  1 pair of chromosomes in G 1 phase of cell cycle; two alleles E & e, so cell is Ee genotype
Draw cell in G 2 of interphase, Metaphase 1, Telophase 1, Beginning of Meiosis 2 (two cells), and end of Meiosis 2 after cytokinesis (4 cells)
Questions to answer | Starting cell has 1 pair of chromosomes
a.  What is the genotype of the cell in G1?  Is this a haploid or diploid genotype?
b.  How many chromosomes are in your cell during Prophase I?  How many chromatids are in your cell during Prophase I?
c.  How many chromosomes are there in each nucleus at the end of Meiosis I?  How many chromatids are there in each nucleus at the end of Meiosis I?
d.  What are the genotypes of the two cells at the end of Meiosis I?  Are these haploid or diploid genotypes?
e.  How many chromosomes are there in each cell at the end of Meiosis II?  What are the genotypes of the cells at the end of Meiosis II?  Are these haploid or diploid genotypes?

Lab 5
Lab 7
Lab 9
Lab 6
Fern Genetics Project

Diagram of Ceratopteris fern life cycle
Adult sporophyte
Heterozygous for polka-dot trait

Fern Life Cycle – Spores
Spores – single, haploid cells
Germinating spores with rhizoids

Fern Life Cycle - Gametophytes
Hermaphrodites
Male with antheridia
Archegonia with egg cells are located here
Rhizoids
Spore wall
Reproductive structures in male gametophytes - antheridia (produce sperm)
Reproductive structures in hermaphrodite gametophyte: archegonia (contains egg) and antheridia (produce sperm)

Phenotypic trait: Chloroplast distribution in the cell
Phenotypes:  green and polka-dot
Green
Polka-dot

C-fern Observations – Follow instructions for Lab 7 (Part A observations of mature gametophytes, pg. FGP-4) We completed part B in lab 6.
Label the structures you see
male
hermaphrodite (green vs polka-dot)
spore wall
rhizoids
photosynthetic cells (they are the ones with green chloroplasts)
chloroplasts (they will be green bodies inside the cells
archegonium (where egg is produced)
antheridium (where sperm are produced)
Archegonium
Photosynthetic cell

C-fern Data Collection – Follow instructions on FGP 5-7 (located before Appendix in Lab Manual)
Complete the following (due by end of lab):
Complete assignment in Canvas module (15 points)
Lab7_Ceratopteris Fern Genetics project assignment.docx [https://iu.instructure.com/courses/2188478/modules/items/30989855]

Microscope Storage
When finished with observations, put your microscopes away – follow guidelines in Appendix A
Turn off light
Move stage into lowest position
Remove slide from stage clips and wipe stage clear of water with paper towel
Wipe the lenses you used with lens paper
Make sure low power is in center position
Unplug; neatly store the power cord

Classical Genetics Simulator Project - Goals
Study common patterns of genetic inheritance for one and two genes
Learn experimental practices and thinking skills for solving genetics problems
Practice important skills of organization, planning, and record-keeping

Review guidelines for thinking through crosses
What are the predicted phenotypes and genotypes for one gene, autosomal traits with
complete dominance (dominant/recessive)?
co-dominance?
sex-linkage?
Complete worksheet problems on canvas assignment

Classical Genetics Simulator Project – Part 1
Complete practice problem and assigned Problem 1
Use practice problem to learn the system and to practice note-taking
Complete assigned Problem 1
Three inheritance patterns possible:
autosomal gene, 2 alleles, complete dominance;
autosomal gene, 2 alleles, co-dominance;
X-linked gene, 2 alleles, complete dominance.
Keep detailed hand-written (not electronic) notes of your crosses and determine mode of inheritance for each trait
Complete Problem 1; notes and answers due by end of lab (15 points)
Each team member hands in a complete set of notes

Strategy for solving the problem
Start with reciprocal crosses to produce two sets of F 1 offspring
Interpret those results –
generate genetic notation
write out detailed hypotheses about genotypes of parents and F 1 offspring
Test your hypotheses - use F 1 progeny to produce an F 2 generation
Interpret those results
Continue to cross 4

Hints for Success
Stop, think, scribble down genetic notation – work through the possible genetics before, not after, you make a cross .
Talk to your teammate.  Help each other understand and learn.
Do not delete any vials of flies.  It is unethical to destroy data.
Make a cross only after you know why you are making it and have recorded what it is, why it is, and what you expect to find.
Get agreement from your teammate before moving forward to another cross.  Make sure that your both understand what you are doing before you do it.

Assignment
See file in canvas file,
Lab_7 CGS problem 1.docx
Due by end of lab.
15 points

Getting Started with CGS ( cgslab.com )
Log in Course ID = iusb
Student account: mathias , delafuente , burke , dunston , or fu (you will be assigned one of these)
Experiment with the first practice problem
Learn how to navigate through information provided
DO NOT begin the real assignment until you
receive an OK from your instructor
Complete the handout for your assigned genetic problem.
You will work in teams of 2 (maybe 3), but each person needs to submit the assignment.

Dr. Constanza de la Fuente Castro
GenSCAPE lab
University of Chicago
Developing techniques to study ancient DNA to understand past populations in Siberia and the Americas
Dr. Rasika Ann Mathias
Johns Hopkins University
Genetic epidemiology with emphasis on cardiovascular/asthma/lung disease and other chronic disease processes
Dr. John Burke
University of Georgia
Studies the origin and evolution of crop plants, with a current focus on adaptation to stressful environments

Dr. Ying-Hui Fu
University of California San Francisco
Research focus uses a combination of human genetics, molecular biology, cellular biology and neurocircuitry methods to unravel human sleep behavioral traits
Dr. Georgia Dunston
Howard University
Part of team that developed National Human Genome Center (NHGC)
Research focus on how interactions between genetics and environment contribute to health disparities in African Americans
